text: Hello everyone, this is Roxen Liu from Oakland, California, back with a video of a repair of a re-recurrent right inguinal hernia.
This patient had a robotic tap right inguinal hernia repair. a recurrence was fixed with a open proline hernia system and the patient has recurred again.
I purposely kept this video a little on the longer side because I wanted to show every detail.
I feel like in this operation there are a lot of points in the operation where something could go really wrong and so I just wanted to show the entire operation and narrate it.

Since it's the time of Olympics I kind of I use the analogy of figure skating. To win the gold, you have to essentially have a flawless, perfect routine. Redo surgery is kind of similar. You've got to make sure that every step you do is well thought out and that there are very little errors.
You've got to maintain good hemostasis.

In thinking about how to approach this case, I have the option of open laparoscopic and robotic. For me, laparoscopic really isn't an option in this case, just because of the challenges of using straight sticks.

Open surgery would have been a good option also, but with the proline hernia system and mesh in the inguinal canal, I felt that it would have been much easier for me to avoid damage to the testicular vessels doing it robotically than open.

In general, in these redo cases where there's preperitoneal mesh, I've been successful every time in preserving the testicular vessels, the iliacs, and the bladder. So I feel very confident with this approach.
The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap, positioning it between the mesh and the abdominal wall, ensuring the mesh remains attached to the flap.
The surgical field reveals a significant amount of intra-abdominal fat, which provides a buffer between the mesh and critical structures during the redo operation.
The surgeon points out the different layers, maintaining a position between the mesh and the transverse salus fascia, also referred to as the attenuated posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon carefully dissects in the preperitoneal plane, placing the mesh between the transverse salus fascia and the peritoneum, while avoiding injury to the inferior epigastric vessels.

The surgeon continues the dissection on the side of the mesh opposite the bladder, testicular vessels, vas deferens, and iliac vessels, ensuring careful awareness of the location of the inferior epigastric vessels.

The surgeon navigates between the transverse salus fascia and the mesh, noting that the inferior epigastric vessels are one layer away. There is currently no visible identification of the inferior epigastric vessels in the surgical field.
The surgeon works broadly, moving back and forth from left to right, as the transverse salus fascia begins to be cut through, revealing underlying layers.

The surgeon identifies a lateral area where the mesh did not extend posteriorly, creating a window to access virgin tissue and enter an avascular plane. The inferior epigastric vessels are observed approaching closer to the mesh.

The surgeon observes minimal scarring laterally, indicating that the tissue behaves like normal tissue. The mesh is placed very laterally but not posterior enough, and the surgeon continues dissecting along the edge of the mesh.

The surgeon identifies the plane between the tissue layer and the testicular vessels, allowing safe dissection while closely following the mesh. The surgeon defines the tissue planes and attempts to reach the peritoneum, similar to the approach in a virgin inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon carefully dissects to reach the peritoneum, pushing away retroperitoneal fat towards the pelvis to prevent injury to the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve and the femoral branch.
The surgeon navigates clean tissue planes, preparing to address more challenging areas of dissection.
So I just kind of go back and forth throughout this case, do some easy stuff and then do some harder stuff. And I always like to go from a known to unknown space.

The surgeon enters the retro rectus plane, which appears unscarred and is located more superficially, providing a safe distance from the bladder.

The surgeon activates the Firefly imaging system to visualize the inferior epigastric vessels, using ICG fluorescence to confirm the presence of arterial pulsations and identify any large branches or aberrant vessels near the rectus muscle.

The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system, which enhances the visibility of green fluorescence, to identify vascular structures. Despite the inferior epigastric vessels not being as visible as expected, another branch is detected near the mesh, prompting careful dissection around the mesh.
The surgeon uses the tip of a scissor to gently touch the mesh, observing how the instrument bounces off the mesh surface. As the dissection progresses medially, the mesh becomes less visible.

The surgeon carefully dissects near the edge of the mesh, mindful of the proximity to the bladder. The dissection proceeds slowly due to uncertainty about the mesh boundary, with the surgeon working within a confined space.

The surgeon navigates the surgical field, focusing on the area around the internal ring and the inferior epigastric vessels. The visual concern is the proximity to the vas deferens, requiring careful dissection.

The surgeon navigates the surgical field, mindful of potential damage to nearby structures. A Cartier instrument is inserted through the left lower quadrant via a fourth port, which had been previously overlooked.
The surgeon identifies a soft, fatty plane between the fibrogastric vein and the mesh, noting that the artery is more adherent. The dissection remains anterior, away from the vas deferens.

The surgeon carefully maintains the correct tissue plane, visibly lifting the transverse abdominus without dissecting, cutting, or dividing it.

The surgeon identifies an opportunity to dissect between the mesh and the retroperitoneal fat due to the patient's substantial fat layer. The mesh is flipped over to facilitate separation from the surrounding tissues, aiming to resect portions of the mesh.

The surgeon navigates the anterior region, avoiding the bladder and femoral vessels, while manipulating the fatty tissue to separate more of the mesh from the abdominal wall.

The surgeon considers leaving a portion of the mesh attached to the inferior epigastric vessels, contemplating cutting a small piece and leaving it behind on these vessels.
The surgeon continues the dissection, mobilizing tissue posteriorly to create sufficient space for the new mesh to be properly positioned over the bladder and psoas area.
The surgeon examines the surgical field to assess the amount of tissue between the mesh and the peritoneum, noting a substantial layer of tissue that needs to be separated from the abdominal wall.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the medial edge of the testicular vessels, preserving the tissue within the retroperitoneal fat associated with these vessels. A small hole in the peritoneum is visible but not concerning.

The surgeon carefully dissects the inferior epigastric vessels, which are visibly close, using robotic instruments. The pulsation of the vessels is observable, and the dissection is kept hemostatic to avoid bleeding, allowing precise shaving of the vessels from surrounding tissue.

The surgeon applies good traction on the mesh, using scissors and cautery to separate tissue without cutting, aided by tension. The surgeon observes a dense scar plate of the transversalis fascia extending down to the pubis and superior pubic rami, noting the presence of soft fat beneath the scar tissue. A piece of mesh is visible in the area, indicating previous surgical intervention.

The surgeon approaches the tip of the mesh, revealing normal fat and a dense scar plate. Softer mesh is visible underneath, allowing for more aggressive separation of the mesh. A three-way Foley catheter is in place due to potential complications with poly hernia system and tap mesh.

The surgeon observes the bladder, noting its rounded appearance and slight pulsation, resembling a balloon with a fluid wave. A three-way Foley catheter is clamped to keep urine in the bladder, enhancing its visibility and aiding in identifying its location.

The surgeon navigates the space of Retzius, located in the retropubic area between the bladder and the superior pubic rami. The area appears scarred, guiding the surgeon to identify the correct dissection plane from medial to lateral.

The surgeon carefully dissects through the scarred tissue, ensuring the mesh lays across the midline. The dissection space is being shaped to resemble that of a primary hernia repair, with visible anatomical landmarks such as the vas deferens.

The surgeon carefully removes the peritoneum and surrounding tissue from the vas deferens, creating tension by pulling medially to facilitate dissection between the tissue and the external iliac vein.

And sometimes hernias in very obese patients with a lot of fat where I need an extra hand just to help with retraction.

The surgeon performs a standard inguinal hernia dissection, peeling the peritoneum back to expose the retroperitoneal tissue. The plan involves placing a six and twelve mesh with significant posterior overlap to address the herniated tissue.

The surgeon prepares to lay a piece of mesh posteriorly while examining the surgical field to locate the herniated tissue. The external iliac artery is visible as the surgeon opens the area to identify tissue that may separate from the testicular vessels.

The surgeon identifies the large testicular vein and artery while continuing to dissect through layers of tissue. Using a peeling technique, the surgeon makes a longitudinal incision in the anterior lateral tissue to reach the layer where herniated fat is located, aiming to separate it from surrounding structures.

The surgeon maintains a natural plane against the retroperitoneum, with visible muscle contraction indicating nerve transmission. The retroperitoneal fat is preserved, aiming to protect the genitofemoral nerve, although the nerve itself is not visible.

The surgeon identifies a large piece of fat in the groin area, preparing to separate it from surrounding tissues. The testicular vein is visible medially, resembling a thin, transparent peritoneum, requiring careful dissection to avoid accidental cutting.

The surgeon uses a cardiac grasper to hold the testicular vessels while separating a large piece of herniated retroperitoneal fat from the surrounding tissue. The fat is visibly protruding through the internal ring, characteristic of a hernia without a peritoneal sac.

The surgeon searches for the tip of the herniated fat, identifying a plane anteriorly and hugging the fat closely. White tissue is cut through to expose the smooth lipoma-like fat, which appears larger than typical lipomas.

The surgeon carefully retracts tissue from the inguinal canal while holding the testicular vessels in the left hand. The large testicular vein is visible, with a clear plane separating it from the herniated fat. The surgeon explores the tissue, attempting to separate a piece of fat that appears distinct from the herniated fat, ensuring not to accidentally divide the testicular vein.

The surgeon observes a complex network of blood vessels, suspecting varicoceles being pulled from the scrotum. A clear plane is visible between the fat intended for reduction and the testicular vessels. Anteriorly, there is significant tissue herniating through, not easily slipping out like a typical core lipoma. White tissue reflecting upwards is noted, possibly the tunica.

The surgeon encounters increased bleeding while attempting to reduce the fatty tissue at the tip. A decision is made to transect at a specific point to avoid injury, revealing additional fat upon pulling. The surgeon identifies the point of transection and carefully dissects through the tissue layers, using bipolar cautery to control visible blood vessels. The surgeon peels back a piece of fat from the inguinal canal to expose the area for mesh placement over the testicular vessels. The procedure involves careful manipulation to avoid bleeding, which can occur during this step.

The surgeon identifies and visualizes the vessels clearly, applying a large wet clip to ensure hemostasis and prevent further bleeding. The vessel is ligated to prevent retraction into the scrotum or inguinal canal.
The surgeon successfully removes a large amount of herniated tissue from the scrotum, ensuring a safe procedure. The surgical field is prepared for mesh placement to treat the hernia effectively.

The surgeon assesses the surgical field, noting sufficient space for a good-sized piece of mesh. The posterior portion of the wall is skeletonized, revealing two centimeters deep past the midline. The surgeon identifies the edge of the proline hernia system within the exposed area.

The surgeon inspects the inguinal canal for additional fatty tissue, noting the presence of fat that appears to be part of the spermatic cord. The testicular artery is visible in the back, while the vein is seen in the front.

The surgeon has peeled back the herniated contents, positioning them behind or on top of the mesh. The surgeon uses retraction with the Cartier instrument to create tension and counter-tension, facilitating the excision of tissue. As the dissection progresses medially, the surgeon carefully follows the fat plane, ensuring a safe approach while avoiding complete removal of the mesh.

The surgeon considers extending the dissection medially to remove a large piece of mesh, identified as a barred three D max. The mesh is cut into strips resembling an orange peel to facilitate removal through an eight millimeter port.

The surgeon observes changes in the fat tissue as it starts to separate less easily from the mesh. Using electrocautery, the surgeon begins dividing the mesh into strips, resembling an orange peel, while smoke is visible due to the cautery use. The mesh, made of plastic, divides effectively with heat when traction is applied. The surgeon's fellow takes over to continue the operation, which is sped up to show the process of dividing the mesh.

The surgeon uses telestration to indicate the placement of the seam on the mesh, ensuring good posterior overlap. The mesh is positioned to extend laterally, avoiding crossing the midline excessively, to prevent lateral hernia recurrence.

The surgeon places the lower flap of the mesh down, noting the absence of peritoneum behind it. Once positioned posteriorly, the upper flap of the mesh is raised, with the surgeon preferring to fold and unfold the mesh for optimal deployment.

The surgeon demonstrates the placement of the mesh, showing good posterior lateral coverage and adequate anterior coverage. The mesh is positioned without sutures, except for large direct hernias where a stitch may be placed in Cooper's ligament.
The surgeon closes the flap over the mesh, completing the procedure. The mesh is positioned beneath the flap, and the closure is performed without additional sutures.
A large piece of fat that was herniating into the groin is visible. The surgeon closes the flap using a 3-0 V-Loc 180 suture.

This was a six inch. We ended up having to use two six inchers. Usually I can close the whole flap with one six inch, but this time we needed two six inchers. So I'm going to edit this part quite a bit just to show you the end result.

So this patient was discharged home from the PACU. He really didn't take a lot of narcotics. And he is currently doing well, a few days post-op. There's a little bit of testicular swelling, a little bit of cord swelling, but that actually improved quite quickly. The scleral mass is definitely gone.
So thank you for watching. And hopefully this video will help you with your next recurrent inguinal hernia.
